channel.title: Blogs on mahmood
channel.description: Recent content in Blogs on mahmood

item.title: exporting from org mode with pandoc
item.description: motivation the need for an emacs-independent exporting of notes arose from the fact that i was using org version 9.7-pre in which exporting functionality is basically broken. pandoc because i didnt want to rewrite the wheel, i landed on using pandoc, it has a huge community and i trust it to maintain org-mode parsability in the long run. there are some missing features in pandoc exporting which is why im writing this.

item.title: dictionary in emacs
item.description: emacs can lookup words in dictionaries, the simplest usage is through the function dictionary-search and confirming with y when emacs asks you whether to use dict.org (the website), i have this bound as a key: (general-define-key :states '(normal motion) :keymaps 'override "SPC s a" 'dictionary-search) you can also force it to use dict.org without confirmation: (setq dictionary-server "dict.org") or if you want to run a local dictionary server, on arch linux you'd need:

item.title: gpg
item.description: encrypt file with password gpg -o <output-file> -c <file-to-encrypt> the argument -o takes the path to the output file (the encrypted one) and the flag -c tells gpg that we want to encrypt, you will be prompted to enter a password decrypt file with password gpg -o <output-file> -d <file-to-decrypt> the argument -o takes the path to the output file (the decrypted one) and the flag -d tells gpg that we want to decrypt

item.title: maxima in lisp
item.description: i ended up giving up on using maxima as a visualization/symbolic math computation library for lisp, it wasnt made with this usecase in mind i guess, and so its an utter nightmare to lisp with it maxima is written in lisp and so it can be used as a modified/extended lisp runtime how to to start the lisp runtime use the command: rmaxima -r 'to_lisp();' can also run this in emacs' slime/sly with (sly "

item.title: mimetypes
item.description: on linux desktops, the default program to open a file using xdg-open <file> is determined by its mimetype (or filetype) notice that most programming languages and programs on linux also respect mimetypes and their handlers to query the mimetype of a file: xdg-mime query filetype /home/mahmooz xdg-mime query filetype /etc/hosts xdg-mime query filetype ~/test.py xdg-mime query filetype ~/test.mp4 inode/directory text/plain text/x-script.python video/mp4 to query the default application used for a mimetype:

item.title: movies
item.description: psychotraumatic movies that could fuck you up emotionally, be it by watching a mental illness take its toll on a character or by seeing them go through traumatic events these movies are very extreme, as there is alot of sadism, perversion and inhumanity i even had to remove some entries from this section because i didnt wanna go too far that said, these movies arent purely for shock value, if thats what i wanted i wouldve gone for the likes of the appalling A Serbian Film and Cannibal Holocaust, but those are just edgy, gross and dull

item.title: music export
item.description: instrumental post-rock/ambient For Now I Am Winter Ólafur Arnalds Young Mountain This Will Destroy You ST This Will Destroy You Golevka The Evpatoria Report Maar The Evpatoria Report Nights of Wonder I Am No Hero Nowhere Now Here MONO Hymn to the Immortal Wind MONO Chasing After Shadows... Living With the Ghosts Hammock Myra Spurv Dust Show Me A Dinosaur ( ) Sigur Rós Takk Sigur Rós In Never Out pg.

item.title: spotify's api
item.description: getting an access token for the api head over to spotify's developer dashboard click the Create app button enter all the necessary information: App name, App description, Website, etc.. for Redirect URI enter http://localhost:5555 hit the terms of service agreement checkbox click the Settings button, you will find your Client ID and you need to press View client secret to get your Client secret, save both of these as you'll need them api authorization first, we need to gain access "

item.title: symbolic logic in common lisp
item.description: logic programming in common lisp logical statements we make use of the symbolic capabilities of lisp, we use quoting and symbols to represent logical statements, e.g. given the following knowledge base of horn clauses: in lisp we could write it as: (defparameter *kb* '((implies p q) (implies (and l m) p) (implies (and b l) m) (implies (and a p) l) (implies (and a b s) l) A B)) *kb* ((IMPLIES P Q) (IMPLIES (AND L M) P) (IMPLIES (AND B L) M) (IMPLIES (AND A P) L) (IMPLIES (AND A B S) L) A B) the choice of the symbols implies, and could vary, but essentially we should write code that deals properly with these symbols based on their names.

item.title: working with graphs in common lisp
item.description: my initial approach for simplicity ill represent a graph as a list of cons's, if we are dealing with a weighted graph, we use the second item in the cons as a cons with the first item being the node and the second being the weight i implemented the helper functions node-weight and node for this purpose an edge is written/stored in the format '(<src> . (<dest> . <weight>)) or simply '(<src> .
